speaker: interviewer
text: Eugene Fried is being interviewed by Lillian Gordon in Cranbury, New Jersey. So when you left Munich, you went to Gabersee DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gabersee DP camp, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Bavaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bavaria, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like? How was that different? Or, was it the same as-- as--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a DP-- it was not a concentration camp. No, that was already-- it was like a-- I would say, like a campus. It was like-- I don't know. Maybe they had-- oh, you know what they had over there before the war? They had, like, people, distorted people, like people that they-- like, sick people, they were there before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a sanatorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sanatorium. But it was not a sanatorium. It was just buildings. You know, like a campus, you see. It was small rooms in there, and that's where we lived. You got a bundle of roomies. There was the four-- the-- the four of us. Five. Was another friend of ours with us, lived in the same small room. And like-- was like a foyer and one room. The room was about, let's say, about half of this room. Just from here to there. Was about, I'd say, by 6' by 10'. We had bunks in there. And we slept in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sisters-- two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters slept inside. And the boys, me and my brother, and a friend of ours slept in the front, in the foyer. And over there, then, we were there for a while. And I-- the ORT came in. And we went training under ORT-- Organization-- what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rehabilitation and Training.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rehabilitation and Training. So I took up tool and dye making. We were there-- there till-- I was working there-- I mean, training in ORT, in Gabersee. They fed us. There was a kitchen over there. They give us-- over there, they give us rations. There's also rations, food for everybody, you know. A slice-- Italian-- also there was Italian bread. You know, the, like, long Italian bread. They used to give us half a bread and soup. It was good food. And then, they give us rations. That way you could cook yourself. The Joint-- usually, the Joint, you know, gave us-- Distrubtion Company-- Distribution-- Joint Distribution--

speaker: interviewer
text: Committee. Committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Committee. Committee, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it's Committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Committee. Joint Distrubition. And they treated us nice. Yeah, we had the-- over there, they had, like, Jewish policeman, like guards. You know, there shoudn't be any disturbance.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you able to leave the camp if you wanted to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Over there, you could leave anytime you wanted. And you could go into the city. The city was Wasserburg. We used to go and walk in there. We used to walk-- it was free. Over there, we were free. Nobody bothered us. And matter of fact, in the back, was, like, still Germans left over from the-- that they were disturbed. You know, like, not all there. You know, people like that. Matter of fact, those people-- the Germans, they killed them all. Those people that were distarted-- distarted, whatever you called it, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Retarded? Were they retarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Retarded. Retarded. The Germans killed them all. They didn't-- they want to have a pure, pure race.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did these people survive, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how they survived that. But they were there. A few. Not too many. Just a few. But then, in the camps, they took gypsies and Gentile, who didn't listen to them, what they doing. But they wanted to-- they put-- they put them to camp, too, a lot. A lot of them got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were in the DP camp, you learned to be a tool and die maker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I learned tool and die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother learn a trade also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother, he learned to be a fine mechanic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters? They-- they learned--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters, they-- No. They were-- no. They were doing the cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't training women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were doing the cooking. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there in Gabersee till November 1947. November 19-- November 19-- of 29th-- no. 1946, I mean. 1946. The end of '46. And December the 12th, we left. December 12, 19-- no, 1947. I'm getting-- getting all mixed up here. 1947, December 12, we arrived here. But we left over there-- Bremerhaven-- we got to Bremerhaven November 29.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947. '47, we went there. We were there for a couple of days, and then we got to the boat, on the Marine Flasher.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the name of the boat. It was a Army boat. You know, the liberation-- liberty boat. Liberty-- they called it the liberty ship. We were on there for-- took us 12 days to get here, because of the hurricane that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how-- before-- before we get you on the boat, how did you get to come to the United States? A lot of people were going to Palestine, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was supposed to go to Palestine also. I was supposed to go with the Exodus. I was registered and everything. I was supposed to go on the Exodus already. And then, the-- the British wouldn't let anybody in. So then, the Haganah decided they couldn't take-- they figured, women and children, they going to let in. So they didn't take nobody but women and children, but small children. So they left us out. We couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took the women and children. They sent them on the Exodus. In the meantime, we had friends from hometown. They used to live with us in Czechoslovakia. They wrote to their uncles over here-- the name of Endorf. Very nice people. They sent them-- they said, if you want us to come to the United States, you send them papers, too. They didn't ask question. They send us papers also. And by that time-- time came, I couldn't go to Israel. So in the meantime, they send us papers to go to the United States. So we came to the United States-- me, my brother, and-- and one of my sisters. Because one of my sister was sick yet from the concentration camp. She was still sick. So they wouldn't let her in right away. So she came after, a couple years after. So we came. December 12, 1947, we arrived to the United States. It was beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got off the boat-- and matter of fact, even when on the boat, I couldn't sleep. I stood up on top of the deck. And looking out, I said, oh, my, New York, looking at New York. But you know, they let-- they wouldn't let in the boat in the middle of the night. You were saying that-- you were saying there was a hurricane on your passage?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a hurricane. That's why we went around some place-- around the Cape or something. I don't know which, but they went different way, because it took them 12 days to get here.

speaker: interviewer
text: A long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually it doesn't take that long. Yeah. Because it was a hurricane and the ocean-- even like this, the waves were going over the boat. There was such big waves. But when we got to New York Harbor-- almost New York Harbor-- you seen the lights. And I've never seen so many cars at night, you know, riding around. I think it was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Westside Highway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Westside. It was the River Drive, Riverside Drive or something. I don't know what it was. But I see, like, cars going like crazy all over. And I never seen so many cars in my life. And then, when we got-- the next day, it was on a Friday, December the 12th, we got off the boat. And there was that friend of ours, Mrs. Endorf. She greeted us over there. And we went to the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hebrew--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew Immigration office.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Immigration Aid Society.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They go-- they give-- they helped us come over. They give us the papers. But they sponsored us. They endorsed. But the HIAS helped us come over. And we got here. They prepared us a house. Two room-- three-- it was three-room apartment in East Side-- East Fourth Street. Second Avenue, then Fourth Street. And they prepared us food, clothing-- used clothing, but it was good. And about three days later, they took us, they got us jobs, me and my brother. And we started to work on our own. On our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you start to work as?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to work as a baker, because I-- I started to learn in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, from back-- back from Mukacevo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from back-- I started to work. Back from Mukacevo, I was learning to be a baker. So I went in a bakery. I worked there for about a year. I was still young. And our house was still-- we used to get together-- all the refugees, they came together. We used to come nights, stay up nights, and talk, and do things-- you know, we had nothing else to do. And I was tired. And the start of 6 o'clock in the morning to work. Then, the bus would come on 5 o'clock, 4 o'clock, 3 o'clock. And once, I was so tired, I looked down the subway-- the subway's coming-- I fell down the tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You fell down into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I fell down into the tracks. I jumped off--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were partying and working all night--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not partying. Just talking with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Just talking, no drinking. We never drink. So my brother says, that's it, no more bakery for you. No more. [? So I went-- ?] so I got a job as a-- in a leather factory, made the pocketbooks, briefcases. And I was there, working there. And then, I got-- 19-- 19-- then, my brother got drafted first. In 1948, my brother was drafted to the Army. When the Berlin Blockade was, he was drafted to the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they send them back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: They send them back to Germany with the Army in the blockade, the Berlin Blockade. They send them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Berlin Blockade.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Berlin Blockade. He was there for about eight months. Then, he came back. He went back to work in that same place. And then, he got married. And my sister, Kim, moved to the United States, and I lived with her on the East Side. In 1951, I got drafted in the Korea war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? You'd gone through the Holocaust in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? You were now asked to be in Uncle Sam's Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be honest, I was very happy. I was the proudest soldier in the Army. I was so proud to wear the United States uniform that I used to fight with the kids in the Army. You know, the kids, they used to holler, the food, it's no good, this. And they used to say, ah, this lousy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, what are you complaining about? You got the best Army in the world. You got the best food in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you don't know. You're a communist. You're a Jew. You don't know nothing. You're a communist. I says, wait a minute. I says, I'm a Jew, and I'm proud to be in American Army. And I'm proud to be Jewish. And I love this country. And I'm proud to be in this uniform. Oh, you don't know. You don't know what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, you see what you getting over here. The breakfast, they used to give you bacon, eggs, and cake, and coffee. What are you complaining about? They don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I start talking to them, give some sense into them, he says, Gene, you know, I don't-- really, I don't know. You know, they were just complaining-- were just like the East End Army. If you don't bitch, you're not happy. That's how they were. And I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go overseas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. You see, what happened, they used to-- I was on the list three times to go overseas. But they were always filling out papers, question, because I was not a citizen. They were always-- they took me off the list three times. But they send me on-the-job training. I was in Richmond, Virginia, on-the-job training. In other words, we used to help out for the-- we trained the people over here to go-- when they send them overseas, they used to train them work on the railroad. That's where we used to work, on the railroad. So I used to do work over here for the Army, too. So on-the-job training. Keep-- so and then, we were back to Fort Eustis. I went there for six months on-the-job training. Then, we went back to Fort Eustis. That's where my-- my basic training was. That's where the base training was-- the base camp was-- the Fort Eustis. And I was there for a while with the Army training for the ship, what they had training. Everything was the Transportation Corps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, they sent me on-the-job training to Huntington, West Virginia. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were stationed onto the railroad, but training for the Army-- shipping things-- everything-- were shipping for the ammunitions, and everything like this, you know. Doing work for the Army, for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: But to tell you something, how people are ignorant. I was stationed on the train, right? And there was one guy-- I was stationed at one train and he was stationed the other train. One day, they transferred me to the other one right next to one of the guys. I didn't know-- I knew the guy just from talking to him. He says to me, Gene, you know, I hated your guts till now. And now, you're my best friend, and I like you. You're one of the nicest guys I want to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, why the heck did you hated my guts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, why-- then, why did hate me because I'm Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I don't know. He says, I just know-- what do you call it-- to hate Jews. But now that I met you, he says, I don't know, he says. There's no different in the Jews, he says, I don't see why everybody hates them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you surprised that there was-- there were feelings against Jewish people in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was surprised. But this the only guy that-- what do you call it-- said to me-- he came out saying to me that, I hated you, because I was Jewish. And then, became a best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in till 1951.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in. And I was working on the train. And another guy-- what do you call it-- a Polish guy, he used to say-- he used to-- my best friend. One had a plane. And he took me for a ride on the plane, too. And there were two Polish kids. And one, he find out I was Jewish, so he kept, like, throw it out to me, hey, Jew dog, Jew dog, Jewish, Jew. I said, George, I says, please. Enough of that. You know, don't-- I'm Jewish. I'm just like you-- an American soldier, just like you. And he kept on doing it. I don't know why did it. I grabbed him. I threw him one side then to the other. He got up. He said, Gene, I'm sorry. I didn't mean anything harm with it. But I just wanted to tease you. I says, I don't like teasing like this. Then, he became a best friend. I went to his home in Chicago. He was best friend after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you reenlist in the Army? Or when--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they said you're finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing I-- one thing I didn't do in the Army. They drafted me. I was very happy. I was glad to do this service. Wherever they would send me overseas, I would go. I would not-- I would not protest. If they would send me overseas, I would've gone. I told the sergeant, the one who took me off, if you send me overseas, I'll go. I'm not protesting. He himself said, you're not a citizen. I said, I don't care. I'm proud to be an American soldier. I'm proud to be serving this country. I'll do anything they want of me. But I never volunteered for anything. They want something, I do it. But not volunteering.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you learned the Army way is not to say I'll do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. I would not-- I would not volunteer, but whatever they want me something to do, I would proudly do it. I was very proud to be an American soldier. Serve the country-- even today, I can't stand anybody knocking the country. I can't stand, because it's the best country in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a citizen now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. At five-- when I came out of the Army, after five years, I became a citizen. I paid my $10 for the become a citizen, and I loved it. And then, when I came out of the Army, I went to work-- and what was it-- in a grocery store first. And then-- yeah, I went to work in New York, on-the-job training as tool and dye maker. And it so happened, he was German. And he had another guy from Germany also, he had working. Me, he let drag the steel back and forth. And the other guy, he let work on machines.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him, I said, look, I'm here on-the-job training for the United States, and they pay you for me being training here. So why don't you let me train on the-- on the machines? He says, well, he says, it takes time, and this and this. And I says, he just came, moved from Germany. I said, you let him do it? Well, says-- give me excuse. I said, goodbye. And I left. So I came over to New Jersey. I worked for my friend, for a fowl and a chicken market for a while. It was poultry shop. You know what it was. Then, before Christmas that year-- I don't-- I don't remember what year it was-- they asked me to help a lady. The lady, she was also concentration-- in the camp with us. Her husband had died. He was sick from the camp, so he died. She asked me to help her for Christmas in the store. So I remained working there for a couple of years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in the meantime, I met my wife, Estelle. It was a Yom Kippur night, a dance. You know, make Yom Kippur dance. And we got to know each other. And a year later-- the same year, really, we fell in love. And I gave her a ring and we got married then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. And when did you open your store in Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when we got married, about-- I still worked for her a little while, about a few months. And then, me and a friend of mine-- the guy who told me, asked me, to work for his aunt, asked me to help his aunt-- him and I opened up a store. And then, my brother bought him out. He got sick. My brother bought him out. And I was in store with my brother for-- in Newark, after the riots-- 10 years over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the riots, we left the store over there. And then, we went to Union-- bought a store in Union. So-- but when we got married, we lived in Newark first. Then, we moved to Union. We bought a house three years after. We bought a house in Union. So we lived there, and my kids were grow up-- they grew up in Union. They went to Union High School. And we were happily married. And I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know you would like to introduce your children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your wife, and your grandchildren to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, my beautiful kids. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So perhaps we can do that right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce your family to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it would be my pleasure. This is my wife, Estelle, for 38 years. And this is my beautiful daughter, the firstborn, [? Lennie. ?] And this is my beautiful-- my best son, Paul Edward Fried. And that is [? Lennie ?] and Mindy, my beautiful little daughter. And there's my son-in-law, Kevin. And then, it's Jessica. My-- wait a minute. There is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Karen.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Karen. That's my beautiful daughter-in-law. And her son, right in front of her, Zachary. And Andrea and Michelle over there, my grandchildren. And Jessica, my granddaughter. And Max, my grandson. And Zachary, my first grandson. And that's my whole beautiful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you certainly have a lot of people to be proud of.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sure do. I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing with us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my mother's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do not point. Just say who he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing up there with my mother, next to her, is my uncle. His name was Eddie Tindel. Eddie. Next to her was the brother, the Dr. Tindel, Joseph. And next, sitting down, is my grandfather. His name was Ignatz Tindel. And right next to him was the youngest brother, Sam Tindel. This is my mother. That picture was taken before-- before she got married. This picture was taken in Czechoslovakia when I was-- and the passport, the book, was made in Gabersee, in DP camp, where I was training as a tool and die maker by ORT. This picture was taken in Germany, in Gabersee. And this is the passport from the HIAS. Before I came to the United States, this is the passport. This picture was taken after the war. My brother on top-- my-- the older brother-- and my sister, Toby, is next to him. And below, it's me and my younger sister, Irene. And brother, William, on top. This picture was taken on the boat, on the Marine Flasher, coming over to the United States. My brother is in the right-hand side. In the middle is my sister. And I'm on the other side, left-hand side. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, say who are the others there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other ones just-- they were just acquainted on the boat. And I have another sister, that she didn't come in the same boat. She came after, because she was sick in Germany. So it was just, three of us are there. This is me taking a picture at Fort Eustis, Virginia, when I was drafted into the Army during the Korean War. This is me at honeymoon in Florida-- me and my beautiful wife, when we got married. On our honeymoon in Florida. This is my three [? grandchildren. ?] [? Lennie, ?] on the left-hand side, is the oldest one. Paul, on the right-hand side, is second. And Mindy is the baby-- the little one. This is a picture of my family-- married. The right-hand side is Max, that wasn't here today. Next to him is Mindy, whenever she was pregnant with Max. Next one standing up to her is Kevin, my son-in-law, and their little baby, Jessica. And my beautiful, oldest daughter, [? Lennie. ?] And next to her is my gorgeous son, Paul, and his beautiful wife, and Zachary-- his wife, Karen, and his gorgeous son, Zachary.
